title.emphasis: Part IV: Intermediary Control and the Specter of Joint Employer Liability

section.para: The analysis of control under Prong A extends beyond Google's direct actions. A critical secondary layer of control is exerted by third-party financial intermediaries, primarily payment processors, whose own content and compliance policies create a "censorship regime" that dictates the terms of creative production for any user wishing to monetize their labor. This creates a complex web of dependency and control that could potentially extend liability beyond Google to these third-party partners under the doctrine of joint employment.

title.emphasis: 4.1 The "Financial Censorship Regime": How Payment Processors Regulate Online Content

section.para: The ability of users to monetize their intellectual labor on platforms like Google Play or YouTube is entirely dependent on access to payment processing services. In practice, this market is a duopoly dominated by Visa and Mastercard, which gives them and the processors that use their networks (like Stripe and PayPal) immense power to dictate acceptable online content.45 These financial gatekeepers impose their own Acceptable Use Policies, which often prohibit broad and vaguely defined categories of content that are otherwise legal.45

For example, policies may ban transactions related to "obscene" or "certain sexually oriented materials," "hate or harmful products," or any activity that, in the processor's opinion, might cause "brand-damaging" or reputational risk.45 These restrictions are often enforced due to pressure from activist groups and a general risk aversion to controversial industries.50 The result is a system where platforms are forced to de-platform or delist creators of legal content, such as certain types of adult-oriented games or literature, to avoid being cut off from the financial system.53 This creates a de facto censorship regime where financial intermediaries, not public law, set the boundaries for acceptable creative expression.51

title.emphasis: 4.2 Content Policies as a Mechanism of Control Under Prong A

section.para: This external system of financial censorship constitutes a powerful form of indirect control over the user's labor, directly implicating Prong A of the ABC test.55 When a platform like Google requires its developers to use an approved Payment Processor to receive funds, it contractually binds those developers to the processor's entire compliance and content moderation regime.32

This control is not merely a post-publication penalty (like demonetization) but a form of ex ante control that forces creators to self-censor their work from inception to ensure it will be monetizable.53 By setting the rules for what can be sold, payment processors dictate the subject matter, style, and substance of the user's creative output. This represents significant direction and control over the "manner and means" of performing the work.55 Google, by integrating with and enforcing the rules of these processors, effectively acts as an agent of this control, making the processor's restrictions part of the conditions of labor on its platform.57

title.emphasis: 4.3 The Doctrine of Joint Employer Liability in California

section.para: In a "fissured workplace," where a company outsources certain functions, more than one entity may be held legally responsible as an employer.58 California's joint employer doctrine is particularly broad. Under the test established in

Martinez v. Combs, an entity is an employer if it does any one of the following: (1) exercises control over wages, hours, or working conditions; (2) suffers or permits a person to work; or (3) engages the person, creating a common law employment relationship.60

Crucially, this control can be indirect.61 A company may be found to be a joint employer if it "exercises enough control over the intermediary entity to indirectly dictate the wages, hours, or working conditions of the employee".62 This framework is designed to prevent companies from evading labor laws by subcontracting core employment functions to other entities.58

title.emphasis: 4.4 The Case for the Payment Processor as a Joint Employer

section.para: Applying California's joint employer doctrine to the relationship between a user, Google, and a payment processor reveals a plausible, albeit novel, argument for liability.

listitem.para: Control Over Working Conditions: The processor's Acceptable Use Policy functions as a set of work rules. By prohibiting the sale of certain types of legal content, the processor indirectly controls the conditions of the user's creative labor, dictating what can and cannot be produced for the platform's marketplace.45 This is a form of indirect supervision over the substance of the work.

Control Over Wages and Method of Payment: While Google may set the revenue share, the payment processor holds the ultimate power to permit or deny the transaction. By de-platforming a user or an entire category of content, the processor exercises a complete veto over the user's ability to receive remuneration.63 This constitutes significant control over the "method of payment," a key factor in both the California and federal tests for employment.64

Economic Dependency and the Fissured Relationship: The user is economically dependent on both the platform (Google) and the payment processor to monetize their labor. Google acts as the intermediary employer, but it has outsourced the critical functions of payment and content compliance to the processor. This creates a vertical joint employment scenario, where the processor is a "potential joint employer" because its actions are essential to the employment relationship.66 The processor is "acting... in the interest of an employer [Google] in relation to an employee [the user]" by enforcing a compliance regime that protects the entire ecosystem from financial and reputational risk.64

section.para: While a payment processor would argue it is merely a third-party service provider, its role has evolved beyond simple transaction processing into active governance of platform content. This deep integration into the business model, combined with the power to dictate the terms of monetization, creates a strong argument that it functions as a joint employer under California's expansive and functional approach to labor law.
